subject: Physics
solution:
The above circuit in the question is arranged like that, so all the resistance are connected in parallel and the net resistance of the parallel resistor becomes, 


1RP=1R1+1R2+1R3+1R4=14+16+112+18⇒RP=4830 Ω

Now the net resistance will be, Rnet=4830+0.6 Ω, so the net power, P=V2R=(2.2)22.2=2.2 W

subject: Physics
solution:
The circuit has 2R and X in series (top branch) parallel to R (bottom branch).
The equivalent resistance is $R_{eq} = \frac{(2R + X) \cdot R}{3R + X} = X$.
Setting up the equation: $X(3R + X) = R(2R + X)$, which simplifies to $X^2 + 2RX - 2R^2 = 0$.
Using the quadratic formula: $X = \frac{-2R \pm 2R\sqrt{3}}{2} = R(-1 \pm \sqrt{3})$.
Since resistance must be positive, $X = (\sqrt{3} - 1)R$ Ω.

subject: Physics
solution:
When two resistances are connected in series, $R_{e q}=2 R$

Power consumed, $\mathrm{P}=\frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{ea}}}=\frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{2 \mathrm{R}}$

In parallel condition, $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\mathrm{R} / 2$ New power, $\mathrm{P}^{\prime}=\frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{(\mathrm{R} / 2)}$

or $\mathrm{P}^{\prime}=4 \mathrm{P}=240 \mathrm{~W}(\because \mathrm{P}=60 \mathrm{~W})$

subject: Physics
solution:
Initially, the Wheatstone bridge is balanced with $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$.
Since the bridge is balanced, the potential difference across the bridge is zero: $V_a - V_b = 0$ V.
When $R_3$ is heated and its resistance increases by 10%: $R_3' = 1.1R$
The voltage at node $a$ (between $R_1$ and $R_2$): $V_a = 40 \times \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 40 \times \frac{R}{R + R} = 40 \times \frac{1}{2} = 20$ V
The voltage at node $b$ (between $R_3'$ and $R_4$): $V_b = 40 \times \frac{R_4}{R_3' + R_4} = 40 \times \frac{R}{1.1R + R} = 40 \times \frac{1}{2.1} \approx 19.05$ V
Therefore: $V_a - V_b = 20 - 19.05 = 0.95$ V

Hence, n=2.

subject: Physics
solution:
In steady state, the capacitor acts as an open circuit, meaning no current flows through the middle branch.

The circuit simplifies to a single loop containing the $2 \text{ V}$ battery, the $6 \Omega$ resistor, and the $2 \Omega$ resistor.

The total resistance of this loop is $R_{\text{eq}} = 2 + 6 = 8 \Omega$.

The current flowing through the loop is $I = \dfrac{2}{8} = 0.25 \text{ A}$.

The potential difference across the parallel branches is equal to the voltage drop across the $2 \Omega$ resistor:
$V = I \times 2 = 0.25 \times 2 = 0.5 \text{ V}$

Since there is no current in the middle branch, the voltage drop across the $4 \Omega$ resistor is zero. Thus, the entire potential difference of $0.5 \text{ V}$ appears across the capacitor.

Answer: $0.5$

subject: Physics
solution:
Resistors $4 \Omega, 6 \Omega$ and $12 \Omega$ are connected in parallel, its equivalent resistance $(R)$ is given by

$$
\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{12} \Rightarrow R=\frac{12}{6}=2 \Omega
$$
Again $R$ is connected to $1.5 \mathrm{~V}$ battery whose internal resistance $r=1 \Omega$.
Equivalent resistance now,
$$
R^{\prime}=2 \Omega+1 \Omega=3 \Omega
$$
Current, $I_{\text {total }}=\frac{V}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1.5}{3}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~A}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& I_{\text {total }}=\frac{1}{2}=3 x+2 x+x=6 x \\
& \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{12}
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$ Current through $4 \Omega$ resistor $=3 x$
$$
=3 \times \frac{1}{12}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{~A}
$$
Therefore, rate of Joule heating in the $4 \Omega$ resistor
$$
=I^2 R=\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^2 \times 4=\frac{1}{4}=0.25 \mathrm{~W}
$$

subject: Physics
solution:
$\mathrm{L}_1=2 \mathrm{l}$ or $\left(\pi \mathrm{r}^2 \mathrm{l}\right)=\left(\pi \mathrm{r}_2^2\right)(2 \mathrm{l})$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}_2=\frac{\mathrm{r}}{\sqrt{2}}$$;$               $\mathrm{R}=\rho \frac{1}{\pi^2}$
$\mathrm{R}_{\text {new }}=(\rho) \frac{21}{(\pi)(\mathrm{r} / \sqrt{2})^2}=\frac{(\rho) 4 \mathrm{l}}{(\pi) \mathrm{r}^2}=4 \times \mathrm{R}$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{R}=4 \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{R}=3 \mathrm{R}$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}} \%=\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}} \times 100=300 \%$

solution:
The formula to calculate the resistance of the original wire is given by

R=ρlA   ...1

When the new wire is formed, its resistance is given by

R'=ρl'4A   ...2

Divide equation (2) by equation (1) and simplify to obtain the expression for the new resistance.

R'R=ρl'4AρlA⇒R'=Rl'4l   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required new resistance.

R'=l44l×160 Ω= 10 Ω

solution:
Let G be resistance of galvanometer and I' be the current passing through the galvanometer produces full scale deflection. If I is the maximum current, and since, G and S are in parallel so, the potential difference between them will be same.

Then,

 I'G=I2S⇒72I'=8I2⇒I2=9I'⇒I=I'+I2=10I'

So the percentage of total current passes through galvanometer is 

I'I×100=I'10I'×100=10%

subject: Physics
solution:
Using Kirchhoff's laws on the bridge circuit with resistances $6\Omega, 3\Omega, 3\Omega, 3\Omega, 6\Omega$ and applying a 1V test voltage:

From the loop equation:
$6i_1 + 3(2i_1 - i) = 3(i - i_1)$

$15i_1 = 6i \Rightarrow i_1 = \frac{2}{5}i$ ...(1)

From the second loop:
$3(i - i_1) + 6i_1 = 1$
$3i + 3i_1 = 1$

$\left(3 + \frac{6}{5}\right)i = 1$

$i = \frac{5}{21}$ A $= \frac{1V}{R_{eq}}$

$R_{eq} = \frac{21}{5} = 4.2 \ \Omega$

subject: Physics
solution:
The basic principle of the potentiometer is that the potential drop across any section of the wire will be directly proportional to the length of the wire, provided the wire is of the uniform cross-sectional area and a uniform current flows through the wire.

Therefore, sensitivity of potentiometer wire is inversely proportional to potential gradient.

Sensitivity∝1Vl∝lV

solution: $\begin{aligned} & i=K \theta \\ & \frac{2}{G+R}=K \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\theta}=\frac{(G+R) K}{2}=R\left(\frac{K}{2}\right)+\frac{K G}{2} \\ & \text { Slope }=\frac{K}{2}=\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow K=0.5=5 \times 10^{-1} A\end{aligned}$
